lesson.menu_desc: Reading a hard customer email + saying no with tact -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Difficult Conversations & Saying No Politely
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patrícia Xavier Lima Iwashita | Aula 18 -- Difficult Conversations & Saying No Politely

reading.rtitle: A Difficult Request — Email from a Key Account
paras: Hi Patrícia, I am writing because my team is frustrated. We were promised the new reporting feature this quarter, and it is still not live. On top of that, our renewal is coming up next month, and I need three things from you before we sign. To be honest, the last few months have not been easy for us.

First, I need the reporting feature delivered by next Friday, with no more delays. Second, I expect a thirty percent discount on the renewal to make up for the wait. Third, I want a dedicated engineer available to my team twenty-four hours a day. For us, these requests are not really negotiable, because the whole team is depending on this tool every day.

I do not want to escalate this, but my CEO is already asking why we still use your platform. I need to see real commitment from your side this week. Please reply by Thursday and tell me exactly how you plan to fix all of this. Thank you, and I hope we can find a way forward. Best regards, Mark.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from a customer renewal email

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this email?
choices: Mark is happy with the platform and just wants to renew the contract quietly.
Mark is frustrated, and before he renews he is demanding a fast feature, a big discount, and 24/7 support.
Mark is cancelling the contract today and does not want any reply from Patrícia.

items: Mark wants the new reporting feature delivered by next Friday.
He writes that he needs the reporting feature delivered by next Friday, with no more delays.
Mark is asking for a ten percent discount on the renewal.
He asks for a thirty percent discount, not ten percent.
Mark says his CEO is very happy with the platform.
His CEO is already asking why the team still uses the platform.
Mark wants a reply by Thursday explaining the plan.
He asks Patrícia to reply by Thursday and explain exactly how she will fix everything.
Which of Mark's three requests can a CS manager realistically meet, and which ones need a polite no?
How can you say no to the 24/7 support request without losing the customer?
Mark is using pressure ("my CEO is already asking"). How do you stay calm and professional in your reply?

listenings.text: Hi Patricia, it is Mark again, leaving a quick voicemail because I have not heard back yet, and I will be honest, my team is getting impatient. I know I am asking for a lot in my email, but please understand the pressure I am under. My CEO brought up the contract again this morning, and I did not have a good answer. I am not trying to be difficult here. If you genuinely cannot deliver the feature by Friday, I would rather you tell me that now than promise something and miss it again. The same goes for the discount and the support hours. I respect a straight, honest answer far more than a vague yes. So please, when you reply, be clear about what you can and cannot do, and give me a realistic plan I can take back to my CEO. Thanks, and talk soon.

Hi Patricia, it is Sofia from the customer success team, leaving you a short note before you reply to that tough email from Mark. I have handled a lot of these, so let me share how I would approach it. First, never start your reply with a flat no, because it sounds cold and it invites more pushback. Begin by thanking him and showing that you understand his frustration. Then be honest about the limitations. If you cannot deliver the feature by Friday, say so clearly, but soften it with I am afraid we are not able to do that this week. Whenever you say no to one thing, offer an alternative for it, like a firm delivery date or extra premium support instead of the discount. That way he hears a plan, not just a wall. And remember to reassure him that his account matters to us. A calm, tactful no protects the relationship far better than a panicked yes you cannot keep. You have got this. Call me if you want to talk it through.
